title: Your Path: C++ & raylib From Scratch
html:
Welcome to your chosen path

You finished Foundations, and you picked the road that builds games from the metal up: C++ with raylib — no engine, no editor, just code, a compiler, and a tiny library that hands you a window and a render loop. This is the path the people who write engines walk. By the end you will not just have made games; you will understand what an engine actually does, because you will have done it yourself.

Here is the arc of games you will ship across these 8 lessons — every one in this single language and library:

  • A bouncing shape — your first window and loop (toy).
  • Pong — your first real arcade game.
  • Breakout — reusing paddles and collision.
  • Snake — grid logic, a brand-new genre.
  • Asteroids — vectors, rotation, screen wrapping.
  • A 2D platformer — gravity, jumping, tiles.
  • A wave-survival shooter — spawning and scaling difficulty.
  • A polished capstone — menu, score, sound, and a packaged build you can hand to anyone.

That is seven distinct genres. Let's get your tools in place.

The three pieces of a C++ setup

Unlike a managed runtime, C++ needs three things working together: a compiler (turns code into a native binary), a build system (orchestrates compiling and linking — we use CMake), and the library itself (raylib).

1. Get a compiler

  • macOS: run xcode-select --install to get Clang.
  • Linux: sudo apt install build-essential gives you GCC.
  • Windows: install MSYS2 and use its MinGW-w64 GCC, or use Visual Studio's MSVC.

2. Get CMake

CMake is the de-facto standard for C++ projects. Download from cmake.org or install via your package manager (brew install cmake, sudo apt install cmake).

3. Get raylib

raylib is a small, friendly C library for games. We will let CMake fetch it automatically so you never fight a manual install. Why raylib over the alternatives? SDL2 and SFML are also excellent — SDL2 is the most battle-tested (used by commercial games), and SFML has a clean C++ API. raylib wins for learning because it has the least ceremony: one function opens a window, one draws a circle. Everything you learn transfers.

Tip.
Stick with raylib for this whole track. Once you understand the game loop here, picking up SDL2 or SFML later is an afternoon, not a mountain.

Project layout & CMake

Create a folder bounce/ with two files. Here is the CMakeLists.txt that fetches raylib for you:

CMakeLists.txt
# minimum CMake version and project name cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.16) project(bounce) set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 17) # download raylib automatically include(FetchContent) FetchContent_Declare( raylib GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/raysan5/raylib.git GIT_TAG 5.5 ) FetchContent_MakeAvailable(raylib) # build our executable and link raylib add_executable(bounce main.cpp) target_link_libraries(bounce raylib)

Now a minimal main.cpp to prove the toolchain works:

main.cpp
#include "raylib.h" int main() { InitWindow(800, 450, "It works!"); SetTargetFPS(60); // loop until the window's close button or ESC while (!WindowShouldClose()) { BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(RAYWHITE); DrawText("Toolchain alive.", 280, 200, 24, DARKGRAY); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Build & run

terminal
# from inside the bounce/ folder cmake -B build cmake --build build # run it (the binary lands in build/) ./build/bounce # Windows: build\Debug\bounce.exe

The first build is slow because CMake compiles raylib once; later builds are fast. A grey window with text means you are ready.

Lab.
Change the window title and the text. Rebuild. Then change the window size to 1280x720 and confirm the text re-centers (it won't perfectly — note that, we'll fix centering with MeasureText later).
🎮 Build This: Toy — Hello Window

This STARTS the arc with a tiny toy. No genre yet — just proof your machine builds and runs native C++ games.

  1. Get the project above building and running on your machine.
  2. Draw your name centered-ish on screen with DrawText.
  3. Change ClearBackground to a custom color, e.g. (Color){20,20,40,255}, and pick a readable text color.
  4. Commit this folder to git — it is the seed every later game grows from.

title: The Game Loop & Drawing a Moving Shape
html:
What a game loop actually is

Every game, from Pong to AAA blockbusters, is the same heartbeat repeated 60 times a second: process input → update the world → draw the world. raylib gives you that loop structure with WindowShouldClose() and the BeginDrawing()/EndDrawing() pair. Inside is where your game lives.

main.cpp
while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // 1. INPUT (read keyboard/mouse) // 2. UPDATE (move things, check rules) // 3. DRAW BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(RAYWHITE); EndDrawing(); }

Keeping these three phases mentally separate is the single most important habit in game programming. Mixing drawing into update logic is how codebases rot.

Drawing primitives

raylib draws with simple immediate-mode calls — you call them every frame; nothing persists. The essentials:

CallDraws
DrawCircle(x, y, radius, color)a filled circle
DrawRectangle(x, y, w, h, color)a filled rectangle
DrawLine(x1, y1, x2, y2, color)a line
DrawText(text, x, y, size, color)text

Colors are structs; raylib ships constants like RED, BLUE, GOLD, or build your own with (Color){r,g,b,a}.

Making something move

To move a shape, store its position in variables that live outside the loop, then change them inside the loop. Here is a ball that slides right and bounces off the edges:

main.cpp
#include "raylib.h" int main() { const int W = 800, H = 450; InitWindow(W, H, "Bouncing Ball"); SetTargetFPS(60); // state lives outside the loop float x = 100, y = 225; float vx = 4, vy = 3; // velocity per frame const float r = 25; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // UPDATE x += vx; y += vy; if (x - r < 0 || x + r > W) vx = -vx; // bounce X if (y - r < 0 || y + r > H) vy = -vy; // bounce Y // DRAW BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(RAYWHITE); DrawCircle((int)x, (int)y, r, MAROON); DrawFPS(10, 10); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Notice the bounce trick: when the ball touches an edge, we negate the velocity. That single flip reverses direction. DrawFPS shows your frame rate — handy while learning.

Tip.
We are moving by a fixed amount per frame here. That means faster computers move the ball faster. In Lesson 5 we fix this properly with delta-time. For now SetTargetFPS(60) keeps it consistent enough.

Centering text properly

From last lesson — to truly center text, measure it first:

center.cpp
const char *msg = "GET READY"; int fs = 40; int tw = MeasureText(msg, fs); DrawText(msg, (W - tw) / 2, H / 2 - fs / 2, fs, BLACK);
Lab.
Add a second ball with different start position and velocity. Then give the maroon ball gravity: add a small constant to vy every frame and see it arc and settle. Remove the gravity again before the build below.
🎮 Build This: Toy — Bounce Garden

This EXTENDS your Hello Window toy — still a toy, but now alive with motion. It is the launchpad for Pong next lesson.

  1. Spawn 5 balls, each with its own x, y, vx, vy (use parallel arrays or 5 sets of variables).
  2. Give each a different color.
  3. Make them all bounce off the four walls.
  4. Display the FPS and a centered title using MeasureText.
  5. Bonus: when two specific balls' centers come within a fixed distance, flash the background — your first taste of collision, which Pong needs.

title: Input & Your First Game: Pong
html:
Reading the player

A game becomes a game the moment the player can affect it. raylib gives you two flavors of input checks:

FunctionTrue when…
IsKeyDown(KEY_W)the key is held this frame (continuous — for movement)
IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)the key went down this frame only (one-shot — for jump/fire)
IsKeyReleased(KEY_E)the key came up this frame
IsMouseButtonPressed(MOUSE_BUTTON_LEFT)mouse click began
GetMousePosition()returns a Vector2 of the cursor

The distinction matters: use IsKeyDown to glide a paddle while a key is held; use IsKeyPressed for things that should happen once per press.

Designing Pong

Pong is the perfect first real game: two paddles, one ball, simple rules. The state we track: each paddle's Y, the ball's position and velocity, and two scores. Player one uses W/S, player two uses Up/Down.

pong.cpp
#include "raylib.h" int main() { const int W = 800, H = 450; InitWindow(W, H, "Pong"); SetTargetFPS(60); float pw = 14, ph = 90, speed = 6; float ly = H/2 - ph/2, ry = H/2 - ph/2; Vector2 ball = { W/2.0f, H/2.0f }; Vector2 bv = { 5, 4 }; float br = 9; int ls = 0, rs = 0; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // INPUT (held keys -> continuous movement) if (IsKeyDown(KEY_W)) ly -= speed; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_S)) ly += speed; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_UP)) ry -= speed; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_DOWN)) ry += speed; // clamp paddles to screen if (ly < 0) ly = 0; if (ly > H-ph) ly = H-ph; if (ry < 0) ry = 0; if (ry > H-ph) ry = H-ph; // UPDATE ball ball.x += bv.x; ball.y += bv.y; if (ball.y - br < 0 || ball.y + br > H) bv.y = -bv.y; // paddle collision (left) if (ball.x - br < pw && ball.y > ly && ball.y < ly+ph && bv.x < 0) bv.x = -bv.x; // paddle collision (right) if (ball.x + br > W-pw && ball.y > ry && ball.y < ry+ph && bv.x > 0) bv.x = -bv.x; // scoring & reset if (ball.x < 0) { rs++; ball = {W/2.0f,H/2.0f}; bv.x = 5; } if (ball.x > W) { ls++; ball = {W/2.0f,H/2.0f}; bv.x = -5; } // DRAW BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); DrawRectangle(0, (int)ly, (int)pw, (int)ph, RAYWHITE); DrawRectangle(W-(int)pw, (int)ry, (int)pw, (int)ph, RAYWHITE); DrawCircleV(ball, br, RAYWHITE); DrawText(TextFormat("%d", ls), W/2-60, 20, 40, GRAY); DrawText(TextFormat("%d", rs), W/2+40, 20, 40, GRAY); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Two things to note. DrawCircleV takes a Vector2 directly — handy. TextFormat is raylib's safe printf-style string builder, perfect for scores. The paddle collision checks the ball's edge against the paddle and that it is moving toward the paddle (bv.x < 0) so it can't get stuck juddering.

Tip.
A great Pong feel-improvement: change the ball's vertical velocity based on where it hits the paddle. Hit near the top, it deflects up; near the bottom, down. Compute (ball.y - (ly+ph/2)) / (ph/2) for a -1..1 factor and scale into bv.y.
Lab.
Add a win condition: first to 5 points. When reached, freeze the ball and draw a centered "P1 WINS" using MeasureText for centering. Press R to reset scores.
🎮 Build This: Arcade — Pong

This STARTS a new genre: arcade. Your first competitive game, built on the input and motion skills from the toy.

  1. Get the two-paddle Pong above running.
  2. Add the paddle-angle deflection from the Tip so rallies get interesting.
  3. Speed the ball up slightly each time it hits a paddle.
  4. Add the first-to-5 win screen with a reset key.
  5. Bonus: add a simple AI for the right paddle that tracks the ball's Y (with a small max speed so it's beatable) — now it's single-player.

title: Collision Detection & Breakout
html:
The two collision shapes you need most

You did ad-hoc collision in Pong. Let's formalize it, because every game depends on it. The two workhorses are AABB (axis-aligned bounding box — rectangle vs rectangle) and circle (distance-based).

AABB: rectangle overlap

Two rectangles overlap if they overlap on both axes. raylib gives you this for free with CheckCollisionRecs, but understand the math:

collision.cpp
bool aabb(Rectangle a, Rectangle b) { return a.x < b.x + b.width && a.x + a.width > b.x && a.y < b.y + b.height && a.y + a.height > b.y; } // raylib built-ins do the same: // CheckCollisionRecs(a, b) // CheckCollisionCircles(c1, r1, c2, r2) // CheckCollisionCircleRec(center, r, rect)

Circle vs circle

Two circles collide when the distance between centers is less than the sum of radii. Comparing squared distances avoids a square root:

circle.cpp
bool circles(Vector2 a, float ra, Vector2 b, float rb) { float dx = a.x - b.x, dy = a.y - b.y; float rs = ra + rb; return dx*dx + dy*dy < rs*rs; // no sqrt needed }

For Breakout the ball is a circle and bricks are rectangles, so CheckCollisionCircleRec is the perfect tool.

Breakout reuses everything you have

Breakout is Pong's child: keep the bottom paddle, keep the bouncing ball, but replace the second player with a wall of bricks to destroy. We store bricks in a fixed grid using a 2D array of bools (alive or not).

breakout.cpp
#include "raylib.h" const int W = 800, H = 600; const int COLS = 10, ROWS = 6; const float BW = 70, BH = 24, GAP = 8, TOP = 50; int main() { InitWindow(W, H, "Breakout"); SetTargetFPS(60); bool brick[ROWS][COLS]; for (int r=0;rfor (int c=0;ctrue; Rectangle paddle = { W/2-50, H-40, 100, 16 }; Vector2 ball = { W/2.0f, H/2.0f }; Vector2 bv = { 4, -4 }; float br = 8; int score = 0; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // INPUT: paddle follows arrows if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) paddle.x -= 8; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) paddle.x += 8; if (paddle.x < 0) paddle.x = 0; if (paddle.x > W-paddle.width) paddle.x = W-paddle.width; // UPDATE ball + wall bounces ball.x += bv.x; ball.y += bv.y; if (ball.x-br<0 || ball.x+br>W) bv.x=-bv.x; if (ball.y-br<0) bv.y=-bv.y; // paddle bounce if (CheckCollisionCircleRec(ball, br, paddle) && bv.y>0) { bv.y = -bv.y; float hit = (ball.x-(paddle.x+paddle.width/2))/(paddle.width/2); bv.x = hit*5; // angle off paddle } // brick collisions for (int r=0;rfor (int c=0;cif (!brick[r][c]) continue; Rectangle bk = { GAP + c*(BW+GAP), TOP + r*(BH+GAP), BW, BH }; if (CheckCollisionCircleRec(ball, br, bk)) { brick[r][c]=false; bv.y=-bv.y; score+=10; } } if (ball.y-br>H) { ball={W/2.0f,H/2.0f}; bv={4,-4}; } // lost ball // DRAW BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); DrawRectangleRec(paddle, RAYWHITE); DrawCircleV(ball, br, RAYWHITE); for (int r=0;rfor (int c=0;cif (brick[r][c]) DrawRectangle(GAP+c*(BW+GAP), TOP+r*(BH+GAP), BW, BH, (Color){(unsigned char)(40+r*30),120,200,255}); DrawText(TextFormat("Score %d", score), 10, 10, 20, GREEN); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }
Watch out.
Our brick bounce just flips bv.y, which is fine for most hits but can act oddly on corner/side hits. For learning it's perfect; for polish you'd compare the ball's penetration depth on each axis and flip the smaller one.
Lab.
Add a lives counter (start at 3). Losing the ball costs a life; at zero, show "GAME OVER". When all bricks are cleared, show "YOU WIN". Count remaining bricks each frame to detect the win.
🎮 Build This: Arcade — Breakout

This EXTENDS the arcade genre, directly reusing Pong's paddle and bounce logic plus your new collision toolkit.

  1. Get the brick grid + paddle + ball running.
  2. Add lives and the win/lose screens from the Lab.
  3. Give bricks color rows that score differently (top rows worth more).
  4. Bonus: a brick on every 12th destroyed drops a falling "power-up" rectangle; catching it with the paddle widens the paddle for 10 seconds.

title: Vectors, Delta-Time & Snake
html:
The frame-rate bug, finally fixed

So far we moved things by a fixed amount per frame. On a 144 Hz monitor your ball would fly twice as fast as on a 60 Hz one. The fix is delta-time: measure how many seconds the last frame took, and multiply movement by it. Then you express speed in units per second, and the game runs identically everywhere.

deltatime.cpp
float speed = 240.0f; // pixels PER SECOND, not per frame while (!WindowShouldClose()) { float dt = GetFrameTime(); // seconds since last frame if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) x += speed * dt; ... }

At 60 FPS dt is about 0.0167; at 144 FPS about 0.0069. Multiply by it and the result is frame-rate independent. From now on, every movement should be scaled by dt.

Vectors as your movement language

A Vector2 bundles an x and y. raylib ships raymath.h with vector operations that make movement clean:

vectors.cpp
#include "raymath.h" Vector2 pos = {100, 100}; Vector2 vel = {1, 0}; vel = Vector2Scale(Vector2Normalize(vel), 200.0f); // length 200/sec pos = Vector2Add(pos, Vector2Scale(vel, dt)); // move float dist = Vector2Distance(pos, target); // handy for AI

Normalize gives a direction of length 1; Scale sets its magnitude. This combo — normalize then scale by speed — is how you move toward anything at a constant pace. You'll lean on it heavily in Asteroids and the shooter.

Snake: a new genre on a grid

Snake is your first grid-based game and a different brain entirely. The world is a grid of cells. The snake is a list of cell coordinates. It moves on a timer, not continuously — every fraction of a second, the head steps one cell and the body follows. This timer pattern (accumulate dt, act when it crosses a threshold) is everywhere in games.

snake.cpp
#include "raylib.h" #include <vector> const int CELL = 24, GRID = 25; const int W = CELL*GRID, H = CELL*GRID; int main() { InitWindow(W, H, "Snake"); SetTargetFPS(60); std::vector<Vector2> snake = { {12,12} }; // grid coords Vector2 dir = {1, 0}; Vector2 food = {5, 5}; float timer = 0, step = 0.12f; // move every 0.12s bool dead = false; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // INPUT (can't reverse onto yourself) if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_UP) && dir.y==0) dir={0,-1}; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_DOWN) && dir.y==0) dir={0, 1}; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_LEFT) && dir.x==0) dir={-1,0}; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_RIGHT) && dir.x==0) dir={ 1,0}; timer += GetFrameTime(); if (!dead && timer >= step) { timer = 0; Vector2 head = { snake[0].x+dir.x, snake[0].y+dir.y }; // wall death if (head.x<0||head.x>=GRID||head.y<0||head.y>=GRID) dead=true; // self death for (auto& s : snake) if (s.x==head.x && s.y==head.y) dead=true; if (!dead) { snake.insert(snake.begin(), head); // grow front if (head.x==food.x && head.y==food.y) { food = { (float)GetRandomValue(0,GRID-1), (float)GetRandomValue(0,GRID-1) }; } else snake.pop_back(); // else trim tail } } BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground((Color){15,20,15,255}); DrawRectangle(food.x*CELL, food.y*CELL, CELL, CELL, RED); for (auto& s : snake) DrawRectangle(s.x*CELL+1, s.y*CELL+1, CELL-2, CELL-2, LIME); if (dead) DrawText("DEAD - R", W/2-70, H/2, 30, RAYWHITE); if (dead && IsKeyPressed(KEY_R)) { snake={{12,12}}; dir={1,0}; dead=false; } EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

The eat-or-trim trick is elegant: every step we add a new head; if we ate food we keep the tail (snake grows by one), otherwise we drop the tail (snake appears to slither). std::vector gives us a resizable list — our first standard-library container in a game.

Tip.
Reading direction with IsKeyPressed (not IsKeyDown) and gating it on the timer prevents the classic bug where mashing two keys in one step lets the snake reverse into itself.
Lab.
Add a score equal to snake length minus one. Speed the game up slightly each time food is eaten (lower step to a floor of 0.05). Make sure new food never spawns on the snake's body.
🎮 Build This: Grid — Snake

This STARTS a new genre: grid-based logic. Completely different from your arcade games — discrete cells and a list-based body.

  1. Get the timer-stepped snake eating and growing.
  2. Add the speed-up and safe food spawn from the Lab.
  3. Show the score and a high score that persists during the session.
  4. Bonus: wrap around the walls instead of dying (use modulo on head coords) as a selectable mode toggled with TAB on the death screen.

title: Sprites, Animation & Asteroids
html:
From shapes to textures

Colored rectangles get you far, but real games use textures — images loaded into the GPU. raylib makes this trivial. Load once before the loop, draw every frame, unload at the end.

texture.cpp
Texture2D ship = LoadTexture("ship.png"); // once, after InitWindow ... DrawTexture(ship, x, y, WHITE); // simple // with rotation/scale, draw from a source rect to a dest rect: DrawTexturePro(ship, (Rectangle){0,0,(float)ship.width,(float)ship.height}, // source (Rectangle){x, y, ship.width*2.0f, ship.height*2.0f}, // dest (Vector2){ship.width, ship.height}, // origin (rotate around center) angle, WHITE); ... UnloadTexture(ship); // before CloseWindow
Watch out.
LoadTexture uses a path relative to the working directory, which during development is often your build folder. Copy assets next to the binary, or use raylib's ChangeDirectory(GetApplicationDirectory()) at startup so paths are predictable. We'll bundle assets properly in Lesson 8.

Sprite-sheet animation

An animation is several frames packed in one image. You draw a moving source rectangle that slides across the sheet, advancing on a timer (the same accumulator pattern from Snake):

anim.cpp
int frame = 0, frames = 6; float at = 0, frameTime = 0.1f; int fw = sheet.width / frames; // width of one frame ... at += GetFrameTime(); if (at >= frameTime) { at = 0; frame = (frame+1) % frames; } Rectangle src = { (float)frame*fw, 0, (float)fw, (float)sheet.height }; DrawTextureRec(sheet, src, (Vector2){x,y}, WHITE);

Asteroids: vectors, rotation & wrapping

Asteroids is the ultimate vector playground: a ship that rotates, thrusts in the direction it faces, drifts with momentum, and wraps around screen edges — plus bullets and breakable rocks. We'll draw the ship as a triangle for clarity (swap in a texture once it works).

asteroids.cpp
#include "raylib.h" #include "raymath.h" #include <vector> #include <algorithm> #include <cmath> const int W=800, H=600; struct Body { Vector2 pos, vel; float r; }; Vector2 wrap(Vector2 p) { // toroidal screen if (p.x<0) p.x+=W; if (p.x>W) p.x-=W; if (p.y<0) p.y+=H; if (p.y>H) p.y-=H; return p; } int main() { InitWindow(W,H,"Asteroids"); SetTargetFPS(60); Vector2 pos={W/2.0f,H/2.0f}, vel={0,0}; float ang=0; std::vector<Body> rocks, bullets; for (int i=0;i<5;i++) rocks.push_back({ {(float)GetRandomValue(0,W),(float)GetRandomValue(0,H)}, {(float)GetRandomValue(-60,60),(float)GetRandomValue(-60,60)}, 40 }); while (!WindowShouldClose()) { float dt=GetFrameTime(); // rotate & thrust if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) ang -= 200*dt; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) ang += 200*dt; float rad = ang*DEG2RAD; Vector2 face = { sinf(rad), -cosf(rad) }; // up = -y if (IsKeyDown(KEY_UP)) vel = Vector2Add(vel, Vector2Scale(face, 200*dt)); pos = wrap(Vector2Add(pos, Vector2Scale(vel, dt))); vel = Vector2Scale(vel, 0.99f); // slight drag // fire if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) bullets.push_back({ pos, Vector2Scale(face, 400), 3 }); // move bodies for (auto& b : bullets) b.pos = wrap(Vector2Add(b.pos, Vector2Scale(b.vel, dt))); for (auto& a : rocks) a.pos = wrap(Vector2Add(a.pos, Vector2Scale(a.vel, dt))); // bullet vs rock for (auto& a : rocks) for (auto& b : bullets) if (a.r>0 && CheckCollisionCircles(a.pos,a.r,b.pos,b.r)) { a.r=-1; b.r=-1; // mark dead } // remove dead (erase-remove idiom) auto dead=[](Body& x){return x.r<0;}; rocks.erase(std::remove_if(rocks.begin(),rocks.end(),dead),rocks.end()); bullets.erase(std::remove_if(bullets.begin(),bullets.end(),dead),bullets.end()); BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); for (auto& a : rocks) DrawCircleLines(a.pos.x,a.pos.y,a.r,GRAY); for (auto& b : bullets) DrawCircleV(b.pos,b.r,YELLOW); // ship triangle around pos, rotated by ang DrawPoly(pos, 3, 18, ang+90, RAYWHITE); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Two big ideas debut here. Structs (Body) bundle related data so a vector of them models many objects cleanly. The erase-remove idiom deletes dead objects from a vector in one pass — your standard tool for despawning bullets and enemies (note the #include <algorithm>).

Lab.
When a bullet kills a rock with r > 20, spawn two smaller rocks (half radius, random velocities) instead of just deleting it — classic Asteroids splitting. Add a score and ship-vs-rock death.
🎮 Build This: Space shooter — Asteroids

This STARTS a new genre: vector-based space shooter, using rotation, thrust, momentum, and toroidal wrapping.

  1. Get the rotating, thrusting, wrapping ship firing bullets that destroy rocks.
  2. Add rock splitting and a score from the Lab.
  3. Load a ship sprite and replace the triangle with DrawTexturePro rotated by ang.
  4. Add lives and a respawn with brief invulnerability.
  5. Bonus: animate an explosion sprite-sheet at each rock's death using the animation pattern above.

title: Sound, a Platformer & a Wave-Survival Shooter
html:
Audio in three lines

Sound transforms feel. raylib's audio is as simple as its graphics. Initialize the device, load sounds once, play them on events.

audio.cpp
InitAudioDevice(); // after InitWindow Sound jump = LoadSound("jump.wav"); Music bgm = LoadMusicStream("theme.ogg"); PlayMusicStream(bgm); ... // in the loop: UpdateMusicStream(bgm); // keep streaming music fed if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) PlaySound(jump); ... UnloadSound(jump); UnloadMusicStream(bgm); CloseAudioDevice();

Use Sound for short effects (fully loaded into memory) and Music for long tracks (streamed). Music needs UpdateMusicStream called every frame.

Gravity & jumping: the platformer core

A platformer is gravity plus collision plus the all-important grounded check. Gravity is just constant downward acceleration on the player's vertical velocity; jumping sets that velocity to a negative burst — but only when standing on something.

platformer.cpp
#include "raylib.h" #include <vector> const int W=900,H=540; int main(){ InitWindow(W,H,"Platformer"); SetTargetFPS(60); Rectangle player={100,100,32,48}; Vector2 vel={0,0}; const float GRAV=1400, MOVE=260, JUMP=560; bool grounded=false; std::vector<Rectangle> tiles = { {0,H-40,W,40}, {220,H-150,160,24}, {460,H-260,160,24}, {700,H-360,160,24} }; while(!WindowShouldClose()){ float dt=GetFrameTime(); // horizontal input vel.x=0; if(IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) vel.x=-MOVE; if(IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) vel.x= MOVE; if(IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE) && grounded) vel.y=-JUMP; vel.y += GRAV*dt; // gravity // move X, resolve player.x += vel.x*dt; for(auto& t:tiles) if(CheckCollisionRecs(player,t)){ if(vel.x>0) player.x=t.x-player.width; else if(vel.x<0) player.x=t.x+t.width; } // move Y, resolve, set grounded player.y += vel.y*dt; grounded=false; for(auto& t:tiles) if(CheckCollisionRecs(player,t)){ if(vel.y>0){ player.y=t.y-player.height; grounded=true; } else if(vel.y<0) player.y=t.y+t.height; vel.y=0; } BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground((Color){30,30,46,255}); for(auto& t:tiles) DrawRectangleRec(t,(Color){90,80,120,255}); DrawRectangleRec(player,SKYBLUE); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

The key technique is resolving each axis separately: move on X and push out of overlaps, then move on Y and push out — setting grounded true only when we land from above. Axis-separated resolution avoids the corner-snag bugs of resolving both at once and is the standard approach for tile platformers.

Tip.
Add coyote time for great feel: allow a jump for ~0.1s after leaving a ledge by tracking time-since-grounded and checking it instead of grounded directly. Tiny detail, huge difference.

Wave-survival: spawning over time

The survival genre is about a stream of enemies that grows harder. The mechanic is a spawn timer that fires faster as time passes, pushing enemies into a vector that home toward the player — your normalize-then-scale movement from Lesson 5.

survival.cpp
#include "raymath.h" // player at mouse; enemies chase; spawn speeds up std::vector<Vector2> enemies; float spawnT=0, spawnEvery=1.5f, survived=0; ... float dt=GetFrameTime(); survived+=dt; spawnT+=dt; spawnEvery=fmaxf(0.3f, 1.5f - survived*0.02f); if(spawnT>=spawnEvery){ spawnT=0; enemies.push_back({(float)GetRandomValue(0,W),(float)GetRandomValue(0,H)}); } Vector2 player=GetMousePosition(); for(auto& e:enemies){ Vector2 d=Vector2Normalize(Vector2Subtract(player,e)); e=Vector2Add(e, Vector2Scale(d, 90*dt)); // chase if(Vector2Distance(e,player)<16) { /* you died */ } }
Lab.
Wire sound into both games: a jump SFX and a landing thud in the platformer; a spawn blip and a hit sound in survival. Add a survived-time score to the survival game shown on a death screen.
🎮 Build This: Two new genres — Platformer + Survival

This STARTS two new genres: a 2D platformer (gravity, tiles, jumping) and a wave-survival shooter (timed spawning, chasing AI). Build both as separate small games.

  1. Platformer: finish the tile platformer; add coyote time, a collectible coin (AABB vs player) with a pickup sound, and a goal flag that wins the level.
  2. Survival: player aims with mouse and shoots bullets (reuse Asteroids' bullet vector) to destroy chasing enemies; spawn rate rises over time; score is seconds survived.
  3. Add sound to both per the Lab.
  4. Pick the one you enjoy more — it is your CAPSTONE candidate next lesson.

title: Structuring a Game, Building a Binary & the Capstone
html:
Structuring a real game: entities & states

Your games have grown past loose variables. Two patterns scale them: entity structs and a game-state machine. Bundle each object's data in a struct and keep them in vectors; drive the whole game with an explicit state enum.

structure.cpp
struct Entity { Vector2 pos, vel; float radius; bool alive = true; }; enum GameState { MENU, PLAYING, GAME_OVER }; GameState state = MENU; // the loop switches behavior on state switch (state) { case MENU: if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_ENTER)) state = PLAYING; break; case PLAYING: // update entities; if player dies -> state = GAME_OVER; break; case GAME_OVER: if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_ENTER)) { resetGame(); state = PLAYING; } break; }

This single state variable cleanly separates your menu screen, gameplay, and game-over screen — each with its own input handling and drawing. Almost every finished game is built on exactly this skeleton.

Splitting into files

One main.cpp works, but for the capstone, split declarations into headers (entity.h) and definitions into .cpp files, then list them in CMake:

CMakeLists.txt
add_executable(mygame src/main.cpp src/entity.cpp src/world.cpp) target_link_libraries(mygame raylib)

Saving a high score

Persist a number between runs with plain C file I/O — no database needed:

highscore.cpp
#include <cstdio> int loadHi(){ int v=0; FILE* f=fopen("hi.dat","r"); if(f){ fscanf(f,"%d",&v); fclose(f);} return v; } void saveHi(int v){ FILE* f=fopen("hi.dat","w"); if(f){ fprintf(f,"%d",v); fclose(f);} }

Building a distributable binary

Time to ship. Always do a Release build — it is far faster and smaller than the default Debug build:

terminal
cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release cmake --build build --config Release

raylib links statically by default with the FetchContent setup, so your single executable has no external raylib dependency — great for sharing.

Bundling assets reliably

Tell CMake to copy your assets/ folder next to the binary so paths just work on any machine:

CMakeLists.txt
add_custom_command(TARGET mygame POST_BUILD COMMAND ${CMAKE_COMMAND} -E copy_directory ${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/assets $<TARGET_FILE_DIR:mygame>/assets)

Then load with LoadTexture("assets/ship.png") after calling ChangeDirectory(GetApplicationDirectory()) at startup.

PlatformWhat you ship
Windowsmygame.exe + assets/ folder, zipped
macOSthe binary + assets/; optionally bundle into a .app
Linuxthe binary + assets/; or build per-distro / AppImage
Webcompile to WASM with Emscripten — raylib supports it
Ship law.
A game on your hard drive is a hobby; a zipped, double-clickable build someone else can run is a portfolio piece. Always test your zip on a clean folder before sharing.
Tip.
For the web, install Emscripten and configure raylib's PLATFORM_WEB target — you get a playable browser link, perfect for showing recruiters. It mirrors the single-file static-hosting style you may already know.
🎮 Build This: CAPSTONE — Polish One Game to Portfolio Quality

This is the CAPSTONE. Take your favorite game from the arc — Asteroids, the platformer, or the survival shooter — and finish it into something you would proudly show.

  1. Refactor objects into Entity structs in vectors; split code across at least two files via CMake.
  2. Add a MENU → PLAYING → GAME_OVER state machine with a real title screen and a restart flow.
  3. Add a persistent high score saved to disk and shown on the menu.
  4. Add sound effects and one music track; pause with P.
  5. Polish: a particle burst or screen shake on hits, clean centered UI with MeasureText, and a difficulty ramp.
  6. Do a Release build, bundle the assets/ folder, zip it, and test the zip in a clean directory on another machine.
  7. Write a short README with controls and a screenshot, and you have your first from-scratch C++ game ready for your portfolio. You did it — seven genres, one language, no engine.
